text.content:
Montage tests create cinematic, collaborative challenges where every hero contributes to a shared goal under pressure. Use them when stakes are high and time is short.

When to Call for a Montage Test

A montage test is appropriate when:

  • The heroes share a common goal requiring multiple skills and characteristics.
  • There are meaningful stakes — something is gained or lost based on performance.
  • There is pressure — a deadline, impending threat, or ticking clock.

Good fits: Crossing hostile terrain before an enemy arrives, rallying villagers for war, performing a complex ritual, chasing a fleeing foe, weathering a magical storm.

Not needed: Routine travel with no time pressure, simple training during a respite, or any task that can be resolved with a single test. You can still narrate these in montage style — just skip the mechanics.

Setting the Scene

At the start of a montage test, describe the scenario and its challenges — terrain hazards, social obstacles, environmental dangers, time constraints. This gives players ideas about what to tackle.

Decide which challenges can be attempted multiple times (recurring hazards like dunes, fire, rapids) versus challenges that are resolved once (a single locked door, one guard post to bypass).

How Rounds Work

A montage test lasts 2 rounds by default (increase only for especially gruelling challenges). Each round, every hero takes one turn. On their turn, a hero can:

Action Description
Make a Test Choose a characteristic + skill to tackle a challenge. Result counts as a success or failure toward the montage totals.
Assist Help another hero’s test using standard assist rules.
Use Ability / Item A clever use of an ability or item may grant automatic successes at your discretion (no roll needed).
Do Nothing Pass. Often wise if a hero lacks applicable skills and fears adding failures.

Once every hero has acted, the round ends and a new one begins.

Key Rules

  • No Repeat Skills: A character cannot use the same skill twice in a montage test. Multiple heroes can use the same skill. You may lift this restriction for prolonged or narrow-scope tests.
  • New Challenges: Each test should target a fresh obstacle, not one already overcome — unless the challenge is recurring (ongoing fire, repeated terrain hazards, etc.).
  • Individual Difficulty Varies: Set the difficulty of each individual test separately (easy / medium / hard) based on what the hero is attempting.
  • Edges & Banes Apply: Use normal edge/bane rules. Clever, out-of-the-box ideas can auto-succeed without a roll.
  • Couch Results in the Goal: Frame each individual success or failure in terms of the overall montage objective. A failed test to ford a river doesn’t mean they’re stuck — it means the delay let the enemy gain ground.

Montage Test Difficulty

Success Limit (SL) and Failure Limit (FL) for the overall montage test. The base values are for 5 heroes. Adjust both limits by ±1 per hero above or below 5 (minimum of 2 for any limit).

Difficulty SL (5 heroes) FL (5 heroes) SL (4 heroes) FL (4 heroes)
Easy 5 5 4 4
Moderate 6 4 5 3
Hard 7 3 6 2

Outcomes

Outcome Condition Victories
Total Success Hit SL before hitting FL or running out of rounds. 1V (easy/moderate) or 2V (hard)
Partial Success Hit FL or ran out of rounds, but achieved ≥2 more successes than failures. 1V (moderate/hard)
Total Failure Hit FL or ran out of rounds without ≥2 more successes than failures. 0V

Total Success: The heroes achieve their goal without complication.

Partial Success: They succeed, but with a cost or complication — the enemy is right behind them, a resource was lost, an ally was injured, a secret was revealed to the wrong people.

Total Failure: They don’t achieve their goal — the situation gets harder and more interesting. Never halt the story on a total failure.

Default Consequences & Rewards

For individual test outcomes within the montage, use the following defaults to keep things moving:

  • Individual Success with Reward: The party gains a hero token (or grant an edge on the next hero’s test).
  • Individual Failure with Consequence: Gain additional Malice at the start of the next combat encounter (or apply a bane to the hero’s next power roll).

Director Tips

  • You can interlude with a combat encounter, negotiation, or other scene mid-montage — then resume the test where you left off.
  • Reward creative, out-of-the-box thinking with automatic successes or edges — no roll needed.
  • If a montage would be trivial, skip the mechanics and narrate it in montage style instead.
  • You can reveal or hide the success/failure limits — whichever feels more fun and dramatic.
  • Introduce new challenges between rounds to keep things fresh (a collapsing bridge, a sudden sandstorm, an ambush).
  • Two rounds keeps things tight. Only extend beyond 2 rounds for a truly gruelling, climactic challenge.
